course: WM203 — NPC Memory Systems (Each Mind Its Own Well)

key_concepts.name: Nested Cognition (Russian Doll) and Tiered NPC Memory
key_concepts.summary: NPCs within world models are AI agents with their own world models — minds within minds. Each NPC has its own Mímir Protocol persistence layer (miniaturized NPC Event Log, NPC Snapshot Store, NPC Personality Lattice) — a private, persistent record of experiences. The parent world model knows THAT an NPC saw something but not WHAT the NPC remembers about it. To manage resource constraints, NPC memory is tiered: P0 (player characters, quest-givers) get full memory; P1 (recurring characters) get compressed memory; P2 (townsfolk) get key-facts-only; P3 (passersby) get no persistent memory. Deep nesting beyond one level (NPCs modeling other NPCs' minds) is the Theory of Mind problem (Lecture 6).
key_concepts.norse_metaphor: Mímir-in-Mímir architecture: each NPC has its own well of memory, within the parent world model's larger well.
key_concepts.architectural_implications: Memory privacy is fundamental — the parent world model's omniscient viewpoint should not be available to NPCs. NPCs must act on their own subjective, potentially misremembering, experiences. This creates emergent social dynamics.

key_concepts.name: NPC Episodic Memory with Subjective Content
key_concepts.summary: Each NPC's episodic memory is stored as a compact event stream (NPCEpisodicEvent) with key fields: event_type (saw/heard/did/felt/learned), content (the NPC's SUBJECTIVE description — not the global truth), emotional_valence (-1.0 to +1.0), importance (0.0 to 1.0), and confidence (0.0 doubtful to 1.0 certain). Retrieval uses a miniaturized NPCReadGate that scores candidates by: importance × mood_boost × recency × confidence. Mood boost amplifies memories matching the NPC's current emotional state (+30% for mood-congruent memories). Low-confidence memories may be doubted, misremembered, or ignored.
key_concepts.architectural_implications: NPC memories are SUBJECTIVE — Rurik the Smith might remember 'Volmarr cheated me' even if the global event log shows a fair transaction. This creates emergent social dynamics: NPCs who bear false grudges, NPCs who misattribute heroic deeds.

key_concepts.name: Social Relationship Memory as Directed Weighted Graphs
key_concepts.summary: Each NPC maintains a social graph — a weighted, directed graph of relationships stored as SocialRelationship objects: subject, object, type (friend/enemy/family/romantic/debtor), strength (0.0 barely known to 1.0 core relationship), valence (-1.0 hostility to +1.0 affection), last_interaction timestamp, and memories list (event IDs of key shared memories). Relationships are ASYMMETRIC — Sigrún may consider Rurik a friend (valence 0.7) while Rurik sees Sigrún as an acquaintance (valence 0.3). Relationships affect behavior through: greeting templates, price modifications, information sharing, and action thresholds.
key_concepts.norse_metaphor: The 'Web of Bonds' — social relationships as the glue holding NPC communities together. Without it, every NPC interaction starts from scratch and the world feels dead.
key_concepts.architectural_implications: Asymmetric relationships create rich emergent dynamics. Relationship decay over time forces active maintenance — players who ignore NPCs see relationships weaken. Shared memories create relationship-specific context.

key_concepts.name: Goal-State Memory with 4-Priority Hierarchy and Aspiration Engine
key_concepts.summary: NPC goals are organized as NPCGoal objects with a 4-priority hierarchy: P0 Survival (eat, sleep, avoid danger — preempts all others), P1 Vocation (daily role — blacksmith smiths, farmer farms), P2 Social (maintain relationships, visit friends, pursue romance), P3 Aspirational (long-term dreams — 'open a second smithy'). Each goal has progress, deadline, dependencies, and status. The aspiration engine generates new P3 goals from: personality (ambitious NPCs generate more), current state (what is the NPC lacking?), social environment (peer aspirations — cultural transmission), and memory (past successes/failures — persistence vs learned helplessness). When P0 activates, all other goals are paused (preemption).
key_concepts.architectural_implications: Goal hierarchies create natural behavior prioritization. P3 aspirations give NPCs long-term depth but raise questions about impossible goals ('I want to become a dragon'). Goal conflicts (vocation vs friendship) create realistic NPC dilemmas.

key_concepts.name: NPC Forgetting with Decay, Misremembering, and Intentional Forgetting
key_concepts.summary: NPC memory is reconstructive, not recordable — each recall is an act of re-assembly that may differ from the original. Each NPC has its own forgetting curve parameterized by: decay_rate λ (higher for simple NPCs/forgetful peasants, lower for intelligent NPCs/wise elders) and reinforcement_factor β (recall strengthens memories). Intentional forgetting has three mechanisms: (1) Suppression — memory is still stored but retrieve-ability is artificially lowered; (2) Repression — memory's confidence is set to 0 (inaccessible to conscious recall but still influences behavior through implicit pathways); (3) Amnesia — traumatic or magical event wipes episodic events (still exist in global event log). Emotional bias: happy NPCs recall positive interactions; angry NPCs recall grievances.
key_concepts.norse_metaphor: The 'Crooked Path of Recall' — memory is not a straight road back to truth but a winding path that can lead astray.
key_concepts.architectural_implications: Imperfect NPC memory creates interesting social dynamics but can frustrate players. Repressed memories still influence behavior implicitly (behavior shaped by memories the NPC cannot consciously access). Players can exploit debt-forgetting NPCs by avoiding them.

facts: Mímir-in-Mímir architecture: each NPC has own Event Log, Snapshot Store, Personality Lattice inside parent world model
Tiered NPC memory: P0 (full), P1 (compressed), P2 (key facts only), P3 (no memory/procedural)
Memory privacy: parent world model knows THAT NPC saw something but not WHAT NPC remembers
Belief divergence: parent has omniscient truth, NPC has subjective belief that may differ
NPCEpisodicEvent fields: event_type (saw/heard/did/felt/learned), content (SUBJECTIVE description), emotional_valence (-1.0 to +1.0), importance (0.0 to 1.0), confidence (0.0 doubtful to 1.0 certain)
NPCReadGate retrieval scores: importance × mood_boost × recency × confidence
Mood boost: +30% for mood-congruent memories (emotions amplify recall of emotionally similar memories)
Subjective content: NPC may remember events differently from global truth
SocialRelationship: subject, object, type, strength (0.0-1.0), valence (-1.0 hostility to +1.0 affection), last_interaction, memories list
Relationships are ASYMMETRIC — one NPC can rate the relationship differently from the other
Relationships affect: greeting templates, price modifications (-10% to -30% for friends, +20% for enemies), information sharing, action thresholds
Relationship decay over time if not maintained
4-priority hierarchy: P0 Survival (preempts all), P1 Vocation (daily role), P2 Social (relationships), P3 Aspirational (long-term dreams)
Aspiration engine generates P3 goals from: personality traits, current needs, social environment (peer aspirations), past memories
NPCGoal has: progress (0.0-1.0), deadline, dependencies, status (active/paused/completed/failed)
Forgetting curve: memory_strength = exp(-λ × age_hours / reinforcement)
λ (decay_rate) varies by NPC intelligence, β (reinforcement) varies by NPC memory quality
Three intentional forgetting modes: Suppression (lower retrieve-ability), Repression (confidence=0, still influences behavior), Amnesia (deletes from episodic store, still in global log)
Emotional bias: happy NPCs recall positive memories; angry NPCs recall grievances
